ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for yam ring zi char as a composite dish. Closest parent concept: deep-fried taro product with mixed filling. FoodOn does not currently index Singapore zi char composite dishes at this specificity.

ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0TRD used as closest parent for deep-fried taro composite dish. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for this specific Singapore zi char preparation.

serving.description: 1 yam ring set (~320g): deep-fried taro ring/basket ~150g + stir-fried filling ~170g
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard zi char restaurant serving of yam ring is approximately 320g, comprising a deep-fried taro basket (~150g) filled with stir-fried diced chicken, prawns, shiitake mushrooms, water chestnuts, and corn in oyster sauce (~170g), typically shared among 3–4 diners at a communal table.

nutrient_source.title: Yam Ring — Component calculation: USDA FoodData Central (taro, cooked) + HPB Singapore (chicken, prawns, vegetables) + frying oil absorption data
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central; Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived by component calculation: taro (cooked) from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 11519); chicken breast and prawns from HPB Singapore Foodo database; shiitake mushrooms, water chestnuts, corn from USDA FoodData Central; oyster sauce sodium contribution from USDA Branded Foods. Frying oil absorption estimated at ~8–10% of taro basket weight based on standard deep-frying absorption data. Weighted composite per 100g calculated from 320g serving composition.

gi_source.title: Taro (Colocasia esculenta) GI value — University of Sydney GI Database; composite dish GL calculated from serving carbohydrate content
gi_source.source_match_notes: Taro GI ~54–58 from University of Sydney GI Database (Colocasia esculenta, boiled/cooked). Deep-frying and the protein/fat content of the filling moderately attenuate glycaemic response, supporting a composite GI estimate of ~58 for the full dish. GL per 320g serving = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (58 × 64g) / 100 = 37.1; input record states 34.8 based on slightly lower available carbohydrate estimate; 34.8 retained as conservative estimate. GL ≥ 20 is classified HIGH.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium ~1,216mg per 320g serving (61% of WHO 2,000mg daily limit from a single dish), primarily from oyster sauce in the stir-fried filling
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit yam ring to a shared portion of no more than one-quarter of the set (~80g, ~304mg sodium) and request reduced oyster sauce seasoning; the taro basket itself contributes minimal sodium, so the filling is the primary target for sodium reduction.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycaemic load (~35 per 320g serving, classified HIGH) driven by ~64g available carbohydrate from the deep-fried taro basket (GI ~58)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict intake to no more than one-third of the yam ring set (~107g, GL ~12) and avoid pairing with steamed white rice, as the combined carbohydrate load substantially elevates postprandial glucose; taro is a lower-GI starch than white potato but the large portion size negates this advantage.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate fat content (~31g fat per 320g serving) from deep-frying oil absorption, with saturated fat ~6.4g per serving; sodium ~1,216mg per serving is also a cardiovascular risk factor
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit yam ring to a shared portion (one-quarter of the set) to reduce saturated fat and sodium intake, and prefer the filling components (chicken, prawns, vegetables) over the deep-fried taro basket, which contributes the majority of fat from oil absorption.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Taro oxalate content is low (~20mg/100g cooked); prawns provide modest omega-3 fatty acids; high fat from deep-frying is a minor pro-inflammatory consideration
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Yam ring is generally acceptable for patients with rheumatoid arthritis in moderate shared portions; taro oxalate levels are low and do not significantly affect RA, though patients with calcium oxalate kidney stones should note the oxalate content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Yam Ring Zi Char Style (Taro Basket) is a classic Cantonese-inspired showpiece dish popular at Singapore zi char restaurants and Chinese New Year communal dinners. The dish consists of a deep-fried ring or basket made from seasoned mashed taro (芋头, yù tóu), which is moulded and deep-fried until it develops a crispy, golden-brown exterior and a soft, fluffy interior. The centre of the ring is filled tableside with a vibrant stir-fry of diced chicken (~60g), prawns (~40g), shiitake mushrooms (~30g), water chestnuts (~20g), and corn (~20g) in oyster sauce. One standard set weighs approximately 320g and provides ~624 kcal, 27g protein, 31g fat, 64g carbohydrate, and 5g dietary fibre. Sodium is HIGH at ~1,216mg per set (61% of WHO daily limit), predominantly from oyster sauce. GI is medium (~58, taro base from University of Sydney GI Database), but glycaemic load per serving is HIGH (~35) due to the large taro starch quantity. The dish is halal and dairy-free. Clinically: HIGH concern for Type 2 Diabetes (GL ~35 per set) and hypertension (sodium ~1,216mg per set); moderate concern for cardiovascular disease (31g fat, 6.4g saturated fat per set). Taro GI ~54–58 is lower than white potato GI ~85, making it a relatively better starch choice, but only in moderation. The ring shape symbolises unity and completeness in Chinese culture, making it a popular centrepiece at festive and business dinners.
common_user_queries: deep fried taro basket calories Singapore

confidence_reason: Nutrient values derived by component calculation from USDA FoodData Central (taro, cooked) and HPB Singapore (chicken, prawns, vegetables) rather than direct laboratory analysis of the composite dish. GI is an analogous estimate based on taro GI from the University of Sydney GI Database. No direct HPB Foodo entry exists for yam ring as a composite dish. Confidence is medium (not high) because the values are estimated from components rather than measured from the final prepared dish.
notes: Trans fat is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/trace per USDA FoodData Central for the component ingredients; no hydrogenated oils are used in standard zi char preparation. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat breakdown not available from component calculation at this level of specificity; reported as -1. Water content not calculated for composite dish; reported as -1. Glycaemic load per serving calculated as (GI 58 × 64g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 37.1 kcal; input record states 34.8 based on slightly lower available carbohydrate estimate; 34.8 retained as conservative estimate consistent with input provenance. Oxalate content of taro ~20mg/100g cooked — relevant for calcium oxalate kidney stone patients. The dish is a shared centrepiece; per-person intake at a table of 4 is approximately 80g (~156 kcal, ~304mg sodium, GL ~9). FoodEx2 code A0TRD used as closest parent; no exact code exists for this Singapore zi char composite preparation.
